narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 68.4% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the university prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes to maintain high pass rates. For context, you're a Black male with a lower-middle family income background, an undergraduate GPA of 2.40—below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 34, which is also under the dataset average of 36.7; you're planning full-time enrollment, like most applicants. The model's decision hinged on three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence. Most significantly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as it signals consistently low performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score, while not top-tier, had the opposite effect, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success because it showed solid potential compared to lower-scoring students. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 1—again well below the average around 5.7—which reinforced the risk by highlighting early academic struggles that often persist. Overall, your position in the bottom decile for both early and later undergrad years stood out as unusually low compared to typical admitted students, outweighing your other strengths and driving the failure prediction. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal qualities, but rather patterns the model has learned from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could significantly shift your predicted odds, opening doors at this or other programs.
